subject: Physics
q: When a hydrogen atom going from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn></math> to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn></math> emits a photon, its recoil speed is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>x</mi><mn>5</mn></mfrac><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. Where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo></math>_______. (Use: mass of hydrogen atom <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>6</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>27</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math>, charge of electron <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>e</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>6</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>19</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mo>)</mo></math>
question: When a hydrogen atom going from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn></math> to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn></math> emits a photon, its recoil speed is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>x</mi><mn>5</mn></mfrac><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. Where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo></math>_______. (Use: mass of hydrogen atom <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>6</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>27</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math>, charge of electron <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>e</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>6</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>19</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mo>)</mo></math>
solution:
According to the above diagram, the difference in energy between the two states is given by

ΔE=-3.4 eV--13.6 eV=10.2 eV

The formula to calculate the recoil speed is given by

v=∆Emc   ...1

From equation (1), it follows that

v=10.2 eV×1.6×10-19 J1 eV1.6×10-27 kg×3×108 m s-1=3.4 m s-1=175 m s-1

Therefore, x=17.

subject: Physics
q: Suppose an electron is attracted towards the origin by a force $k / r$ where ' $k$ ' is a constant and ' $r$ ' is the distance of the electron from the origin. By applying Bohr model to this system, the radius of the $n^{\text {th }}$ orbital of the electron is found to be ' $r_n$ ' and the kinetic energy of the electron to be $T_n$. Then which of the following is true?
question: Suppose an electron is attracted towards the origin by a force $k / r$ where ' $k$ ' is a constant and ' $r$ ' is the distance of the electron from the origin. By applying Bohr model to this system, the radius of the $n^{\text {th }}$ orbital of the electron is found to be ' $r_n$ ' and the kinetic energy of the electron to be $T_n$. Then which of the following is true?
solution:
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \frac{\mathrm{k}}{\mathrm{r}}=\frac{m v^2}{\mathrm{r}} \\
& \mathrm{mv}=\mathrm{k} \quad \text { (independent or } r \text { ) } \\
& \mathrm{n}\left(\frac{\mathrm{h}}{2 \pi}\right)=m v r \Rightarrow r \propto \mathrm{n} \text { and } \mathrm{T}=\frac{1}{2} m v^2 \text { is independent of } \mathrm{n} .
\end{aligned}
$$

subject: Physics
q: The angular momentum for the electron in Bohr’s orbit is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>L</mi></math>. If the electron is assumed to revolve in second orbit of hydrogen atom, then the change in angular momentum will be
question: The angular momentum for the electron in Bohr’s orbit is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>L</mi></math>. If the electron is assumed to revolve in second orbit of hydrogen atom, then the change in angular momentum will be
solution:
Bohr's formula for angular momentum of an electron in an orbit is given by 

L=nh2π

For the first orbit, n=1 and the angular momentum is L1=h2π=L

For 2nd orbit

L2=2h2π=2L

Hence, the change in angular momentum is 

ΔL=2L-L=L

q: The ratio of wavelength of spectral lines <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mi>α</mi></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mi>β</mi></msub></math> in the Balmer series is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>x</mi><mn>20</mn></mfrac></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is _____.
question: The ratio of wavelength of spectral lines <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mi>α</mi></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mi>β</mi></msub></math> in the Balmer series is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>x</mi><mn>20</mn></mfrac></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is _____.
solution:
The formula to calculate the wavelength of Hα-line in Balmer series is given by

hcλα=13.614-19   ...1

The formula to calculate the wavelength of Hβ-line in Balmer series is given by

hcλβ=13.614-116   ...2

Divide equation (2) by equation (1) 
⇒λαλβ=14-11614-19
=3664×125
=2720

q: If the wavelength of the first member of Lyman series of hydrogen is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>λ</mi></math>. The wavelength of the second member will be
question: If the wavelength of the first member of Lyman series of hydrogen is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>λ</mi></math>. The wavelength of the second member will be
solution:
For Lyman series:

For first member, 1λ=13.6Z2hc112-122 ….. (i)

For second member, 1λ'=13.6Z2hc112-132 ….. (ii)

On dividing (i) and (ii), we get

λ'=2732λ

subject: Physics
q: A hydrogen atom, initially in the ground state is excited by absorbing a photon of wavelength $980 Ã…$. The radius of the atom in the excited state, in terms of Bohr radius $a_{0}$, will be:
question: A hydrogen atom, initially in the ground state is excited by absorbing a photon of wavelength $980 Ã…$. The radius of the atom in the excited state, in terms of Bohr radius $a_{0}$, will be:
solution:
Energy of photon $=\frac{\mathrm{hc}}{\lambda}=\frac{12500}{980}=12.75 \mathrm{eV}$

Energy of electron in $\mathrm{n}^{\text {th }}$ orbit is given by

$\mathrm{En}=\frac{-13.6}{\mathrm{n}^{2}} \Rightarrow \mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{n}}-\mathrm{E}_{1}=-13.6\left[\frac{1}{\mathrm{n}^{2}} \frac{-1}{1^{2}}\right]$

$\Rightarrow 12.75=13.6\left[\frac{1}{1^{2}} \frac{-1}{\mathrm{n}^{2}}\right] \Rightarrow \mathrm{n}=4$

$\therefore$ Electron will excite to $\mathrm{n}=4$ We know that ' $R^{\prime} \propto n^{2}$

$\therefore$ Radius of atom will be $16 \mathrm{a}_{0}$

question: The recoil speed of a hydrogen atom after it emits a photon in going from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>5</mn></math> state to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>5</mn></math> state will be
solution:

=13.6-0.54 eV=13.06 eV

Pi=Pf (By linear momentum conservation)

0=hλ-Mv=VRecoil=hλM        ...1

& ΔE=hcλ=hcλM×M⇒McVRecoil

VRecoil =ΔEMc=13.06×1.6×10-191.67×10-27×3×108=4.17 m s-1

subject: Physics
q: Which level of the single ionized carbon has the same energy as the ground state energy of hydrogen atom?
question: Which level of the single ionized carbon has the same energy as the ground state energy of hydrogen atom?
solution:
Energy of H -atom is E=-13.6Z2/n2 for H-atom Z=1 and for ground state, n=1

⇒E=-13.6×1212=-13.6 eV

Now for carbon atom (single ionised), Z=6

E=-13.6Z2n2=-13.6 (given)

⇒n2=62⇒n=6

question: If an electron is moving in the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>n</mi><mtext>th </mtext></msup></math> orbit of the hydrogen atom, then its velocity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mstyle mathvariant="italic"><mo>(</mo><msub><mi>v</mi><mi>n</mi></msub><mo>)</mo></mstyle></math> for the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>n</mi><mtext>th </mtext></msup></math> orbit is given as:
solution:
We know velocity of electron in nth  shell of hydrogen atom is given by

v=2πkZe2nh

∴v∝1n
